slides.text: A cluster is only as useful as your ability to fill it. The wrong pattern leaves workers idle; the right one turns N nodes into ~N× throughput.

slides.title: What we'll cover
items: Match the pattern to the workload's shape
Pandas Function APIs: per-group training & batch scoring

slides.title: Shape drives the pattern
slides.subtitle: Pick the tool that matches your workload

slides.text: The mistake to avoid: .collect() or .toPandas() a big dataset and loop in plain Python. That uses ONE core while the cluster sits idle.

steps.title: Partitions
steps.description: The DataFrame is split into partitions.

steps.title: Cores
steps.description: Tasks run on executor cores in parallel.

steps.title: Tune
steps.description: Target ~2–4× total cores; AQE auto-tunes shuffles.

slides.title: applyInPandas — one model per group
bullets: Split-apply-combine over a group key
Each group arrives as a pandas DataFrame
Use sklearn / statsmodels / prophet inside
Hundreds of models train concurrently
⚠️ Whole group loads in memory — watch skew
slides.card_content:
df.groupBy('store_id') \
  .applyInPandas(
     fit_group,
     schema=out_schema)

# fit_group runs on an executor,
# once per group.

slides.title: mapInPandas — distributed batch inference
slides.card_content:
df.mapInPandas(
   predict_batches,
   schema=out_schema)

# model loaded ONCE per
# partition, vectorized
# over each batch
bullets: Streams batches of rows per partition
Load the model once, not once per row
Vectorize prediction over each batch
Iterator in → iterator out (memory control)
Every core scores its share of the table

slides.title: The current tuning stack
right: Ray backend, built into DBR ML

slides.text: Hyperopt is deprecated — no longer maintained and removed from Databricks Runtime ML after 16.4 LTS. Migrate SparkTrials/Hyperopt to Optuna or Ray Tune.

slides.title: Distribute Optuna trials with the Spark backend
slides.content:
from joblibspark import register_spark
import joblib, optuna
register_spark()

with joblib.parallel_backend('spark', n_jobs=-1):
    study = optuna.create_study(direction='maximize')
    study.optimize(objective, n_trials=50, n_jobs=-1)
# Independent trials dispatched to executors.

slides.title: joblib-spark backend
bullets: Many sklearn tools parallelize via Joblib
GridSearchCV, RandomForest, CV folds
Redirect that parallelism onto Spark
No rewrite — just a context manager
Driver-bound search → cluster-wide
slides.card_content:
register_spark()
with joblib.parallel_backend(
        'spark', n_jobs=-1):
    grid.fit(X, y)

# each (params × fold)
# is an executor task

slides.title: Distributed MLlib & GPUs
slides.subtitle: One big model; filling GPU hardware

slides.title: Spark MLlib for one big model
GPU task scheduling
pros: One task owns all node GPUs

items.text: Never loop over collected data on the driver
Watch for skew — uneven groups idle some executors, OOM others
Match the pattern to the workload's shape
Watch the Spark UI for stragglers, spill, idle cores

slides.title: Key takeaways
bullets: Parallelism is about filling the cluster — match pattern to shape
applyInPandas turns single-node libraries into per-group training
mapInPandas gives efficient distributed batch inference
Optuna + Ray Tune are the tuning stack (Hyperopt is deprecated)
joblib-spark distributes scikit-learn with almost no code change
MLlib / pyspark.ml.connect trains one big model on distributed data
